item.title: Beating the Crowds: A Smarter Way to Do America's National Parks in Peak Summer
item.body:
Late June flips the switch on the American summer. School's out, the days are long, and the country's most famous national parks — Yosemite, Zion, the Grand Canyon, Yellowstone — fill to the brim. The scenery is worth it. The parking lot at 11 a.m. is not.

Here's how to have the wild to yourself even in the busiest weeks of the year.

## Go early, or go late

The single best trick costs nothing: arrive before 8 a.m. or after 4 p.m. Trailheads that are gridlocked at midday are nearly empty at dawn, the light is better for photos, and the summer heat hasn't peaked. Many parks now require timed-entry reservations in summer — book them the moment they open.

## Trade the icon for its neighbor

For every headline park, there's a quieter twin nearby:

- Instead of **Zion's** packed canyon, try the red-rock trails of nearby state parks.
- Instead of **Yosemite Valley**, head to the high country meadows once the snow clears.
- Instead of the **Grand Canyon's** South Rim, consider the cooler, emptier North Rim.

## Pack for the desert, even when it's not the desert

Summer in the American West is a heat game. Carry more water than you think you need — a gallon per person on a full day of hiking is not excessive. Add electrolytes, a wide-brim hat, and real sun protection. Start big climbs before the sun clears the ridgeline.

## Respect the "leave no trace" basics

- Stay on the trail; shortcutting switchbacks scars the land for decades.
- Keep your distance from wildlife — a bison is not a photo prop.
- Pack out everything, peels and cores included.

The parks belong to everyone, which is exactly why they feel crowded this week. Shift your clock a few hours, wander one valley over, and the busiest season of the year can still feel like it's just you and the view.
